does: The paper presents an unsupervised algorithm for extracting and eliciting latent skills from pretrained language models without requiring labeled data or task-specific supervision. The method operates directly on pretrained models and discovers distinct behavioral capabilities by analyzing the model's outputs and internal representations. Rather than fine-tuning on annotated datasets, the algorithm uses self-supervised signals to identify when a model possesses specific competencies and automatically prompts or conditions the model to reliably express those skills. The core contribution is demonstrating that rich behavioral capacities exist implicitly in pretrained models and can be systematically surfaced through unsupervised probing and activation techniques.
connects: This work builds on the extensive literature showing that pretrained language models contain rich internal knowledge and capabilities that are not always reliably expressed through standard prompting or use. It extends prior research on mechanistic interpretability, skill discovery, and behavioral analysis of large language models by proposing an automated, annotation-free approach. The research connects to work on prompt engineering and in-context learning by exploring how to optimally elicit model behaviors, while relating to unsupervised representation learning techniques that discover structure without labeled examples. It addresses longstanding questions in the field about whether model capabilities are limited by instruction-following ability versus fundamental capacity, and builds on methods for probing pretrained representations.
enables: This work opens pathways toward more efficient skill discovery in future language models, reducing reliance on expensive human annotation for capability assessment and elicitation. It could facilitate better model evaluation by automatically surfacing hidden competencies that might otherwise go undetected. The method enables researchers to understand pretrained models more systematically and could inform model training procedures by identifying which skills emerge naturally at different scales. Future applications include automated discovery of specialized behaviors in multimodal or multilingual models, development of more effective prompting strategies based on mechanistic understanding, and creation of efficient fine-tuning curricula that build on discovered latent skills rather than assuming starting from scratch.
